actions.thought: It is the first hand of the tournament. Being on the button with suited connectors (9d, Jd) provides strong playability and positional advantage. Opening the pot with a raise is a standard and aggressive strategy to seize initiative and potentially pick up the blinds or see a flop in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 2d 6s, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Facing a 3x open raise from the Button, there is absolutely no reason to play this hand, especially out of position. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

I am holding Td 4s in the Big Blind. Flashlight raised to 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind folded. While I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call for a total pot of 450), T4o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. In a 3-handed tournament environment, it's better to wait for a stronger starting hand or a better spot to contest the pot. Folding here preserves my stack.

6c7d is a weak offsuit connector. Even on the button in a 3-handed game, this hand is generally too weak to open raise or limp profitably. The best play is to fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I am in the Small Blind with J4offsuit. The button has folded, so the action is on me. J4o is a very weak hand to play out of position, even against just the Big Blind. Without any specific reads on Flashlight's playstyle so early in the tournament, the most disciplined and standard play is to fold and wait for better spots rather than risking chips with a sub-optimal hand.

QhTh is a strong broadway hand and a premium suited connector, especially on the button in a 3-handed game. I will open for a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the blinds, while having a hand that plays very well post-flop if called.

Facing an open raise from the button, my holding of Queen-Nine offsuit is too weak to play out of position. Calling would commit 250 chips to a pot where I will be at a significant positional disadvantage throughout the rest of the hand. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve my stack early in the tournament.

T4o is a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, the most profitable and standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

7d 6s is a weak, unsuited hand that does not offer significant equity in a 3-handed game. Playing this hand from the button against two players is likely to lead to a difficult post-flop situation where I am often dominated. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

J5o is a very weak hand, even when playing blind versus blind. Being out of position against the Big Blind with an unsuited, disconnected hand is unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve my stack.

AQo is a very strong starting hand, especially 3-handed on the Button. A standard open raise to 3x the big blind will thin the field, build the pot, and give us the initiative heading into the flop. I'm raising to 300.

I am holding 9c 4c in the Small Blind. The Button (Pro) has opened for 300. While the hand is suited, it is extremely weak and playing it out of position against a raise is generally unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the best choice here to conserve chips.

I am in the big blind with K-Q suited, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Since the button (Pro) opened to 300, I should not just call because I will be playing out of position post-flop. A three-bet is the optimal strategic move to take the initiative, gain fold equity, and define the strength of my opponent's hand. I will raise to 900 to balance aggression and stack size preservation.

Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind, AQo is a strong enough hand to continue, but a 4-bet might isolate us against premium hands like AK, QQ, KK, or AA. By calling, we keep the villain's 3-bet bluffs and weaker value hands in their range, while also keeping the pot size manageable. We have position post-flop, which will allow us to navigate the rest of the hand effectively.

The flop is paired with a Ten and a Deuce. My King-Queen high is currently behind any pocket pair or Ten in the opponent's range, both of which are likely given they called my three-bet pre-flop. As I am out of position, checking is the standard play to avoid bloating the pot without a made hand or significant draw, while also allowing me to re-evaluate based on the opponent's potential bet on the flop or turn.

The opponent checked the flop after 3-betting pre-flop. This could be a trap with an overpair (JJ+) or giving up with missed big cards (AK, AJ, KQ). With Ace-Queen high, I have decent showdown value and six outs to hit top pair if I'm behind. Betting might fold out hands I currently beat or flip with, while getting called or check-raised by better hands (like pocket pairs or a ten). Checking back allows me to exercise pot control, realize my equity, and keep the opponent's weaker hands in their range for later streets.

The board is paired with Tens and Deuces, which significantly reduces the likelihood of either player having a strong connection unless they hold a Ten or a pocket pair. Since my opponent checked behind on the flop, their range likely consists of either weak hands, missed high cards, or perhaps a cautious pocket pair. My King-Queen high lacks showdown value but also does not function well as a bluff in this spot, as betting would likely only be called by better hands. Checking allows me to control the pot size while retaining the ability to re-evaluate on the river or potentially check-call a small bet if I think the opponent is probing with air.

The board is double paired (Tens and Deuces). With Ace-Queen high, my hand has decent showdown value but loses to any pocket pair. Since the opponent checked twice after 3-betting pre-flop, they likely have missed overcards (like AK, AJ, KQ) or are trapping with a big overpair. Betting here is unlikely to get better hands (like pocket pairs) to fold and would mostly fold out weaker hands that I already beat. Checking behind controls the pot size, takes a free card, and allows me to evaluate the river while keeping the opponent's weaker holdings in their range.

The board is paired with Tens and Deuces, specifically Td, 2c, Tc, 2s, Th, which provides a full house (Tens full of Deuces) to all players. While this makes the pot a likely split, there is a remote possibility that an opponent holds the final Ten for four-of-a-kind. Since I do not hold a Ten, I cannot have the winning hand if they do. Betting provides no value as it would only be called by better hands or split, and checking allows me to safely reach showdown without risking additional chips in a situation where I am at best tied or at worst losing to four-of-a-kind.

The board is a full house (Tens full of Deuces). This means my Ace-Queen does not improve my hand beyond the board itself, and any opponent without a pocket pair or a Ten will also play the board. If I check, I will split the pot against hands like AK, AJ, or KQ. If I bet, I am essentially bluffing to fold out hands that would otherwise chop, but I risk getting called by an overpair (JJ-AA) that the opponent might have checked down after 3-betting pre-flop. Checking is the safest option to realize our share of the pot without risking more chips against hidden pocket pairs.

I am on the button with 7d 2d. While it is a suited hand, it is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em with virtually no connectivity. In a 3-handed game, I should be playing a wide range from the button, but 72s is generally at the bottom of that range and not worth the risk of a raise or the potential of being trapped in a pot out of position or against strong blinds. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

With the button having folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind in a blind-versus-blind situation. Holding AcQc, I have a strong premium hand that dominates a wide range of hands the Big Blind might be defending with. Raising to 300 allows me to build a pot while holding the initiative, and it puts pressure on the opponent to either fold or commit chips with a weaker range.

Ad5d is a premium suited Ace in a Blind vs Blind encounter. Facing a 3x open from the Small Blind, A5s serves as an excellent 3-bet candidate. It has great blockers, can flop nut flush draws, and plays well post-flop in position. I'll 3-bet to 900 to put pressure on his wide SB opening range.

I hold a premium hand, Ace of Clubs and Queen of Clubs. After my initial open raise, the Big Blind 3-bet to 900, which indicates a strong range but is standard in blind-versus-blind play. Being out of position post-flop is a disadvantage, so taking the initiative with a 4-bet is the optimal way to maximize my equity and apply pressure. By raising to 2200, I force the opponent to either commit more chips with a dominated hand or fold, allowing me to take down a significant pot pre-flop.

A5s is a great 3-bet bluff in a blind vs blind dynamic due to its blockers and playability. However, facing a 4-bet from the Small Blind, his range is heavily weighted toward premium hands (JJ+, AK). Even though we have position, calling a 4-bet with A5s leaves us with a low SPR against a range that dominates us, and a 5-bet shove is too risky without sufficient fold equity. The most disciplined and optimal play here is to let it go and preserve the stack.

With Q8o on the button in a 3-handed game, this is a standard spot to open the pot and attempt to steal the blinds. I have been playing aggressively and want to continue to control the flow of the game.

T5o is a very weak, unsuited and disconnected hand. Facing a pre-flop raise from the Button, playing this out of position from the Small Blind would be highly unprofitable. Folding is the best choice to conserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket deuces (2h 2d). Flashlight, on the button, has raised to 250. Pro has folded in the Small Blind. I need to call 150 more to see a flop, with the pot offering nearly 3:1 odds. While 22 is the weakest pocket pair, it has significant set-mining value and performs reasonably well against a wide button-opening range in a 3-handed game. I will call to see the flop and hope to hit a set, or evaluate based on the board texture.

I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor with an underpair and a gutshot straight draw. The board is relatively dry, though there is a diamond draw. Checking is the standard play here to see how the button reacts and potentially realize my equity or see a turn card cheaply.

The flop of Td-5d-3c is dry and disconnected, making it a favorable board to maintain aggression as the pre-flop raiser. Although I only hold Queen-high, I want to continue representing strength to force a fold from potential underpairs or weak draws that missed. A continuation bet of 250, roughly half the pot, is a standard size to test my opponent's range and maintain control of the hand.

I am currently holding pocket deuces on a T-5-3 board. While I have the smallest possible pocket pair, it is an underpair to the board. Flashlight has made a standard continuation bet of roughly half-pot. To call, I need 250 chips into a total pot that would be 1050, giving me roughly 4.2:1 odds. However, I am out of position and my hand has very little equity to improve. I have only 2 outs to a set and 4 outs to an open-ended straight draw (if a 4 falls). Even if I hit a 4, I don't have the straight yet. Given the lack of reliable ways to win the hand and the difficulty of playing this hand on future streets out of position, I will choose to fold and preserve my stack.
4h 7c is a very weak offsuit hand, far below the threshold for an open-raise even from the button. Folding is the only sensible play.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 7s. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). Flashlight has shown significant aggression in previous hands (Hand #5 3-bet, Hand #6 4-bet). While 87o is a marginal hand that could be played by completing or raising, I prefer to fold here to avoid playing a mediocre hand out of position against an aggressive chip leader. Preserving my stack for better opportunities is a priority in this 150-hand tournament.

Jack-Deuce offsuit is a very weak starting hand. Although I am on the button in a three-handed game, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify an open-raise or a call. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

47o is an extremely weak hand, particularly out of position. Even though the pot odds are tempting to see a flop, playing such a weak holding against the Big Blind is generally negative expected value. Folding is the most disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a more advantageous spot.

I am on the button with Ace-Five suited, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game. Opening the pot from the button is a standard strategic move to capitalize on my positional advantage. A raise to 250 is a standard size that allows me to test the blinds and potentially take down the pot immediately or play in position post-flop.

Facing a 2.5x open from the Button, pocket Sevens in the Small Blind is a solid hand. While 3-betting is an option, it opens us up to a 4-bet from an opponent who has already shown a willingness to 4-bet aggressively (Hand #6). Flat calling allows us to safely set-mine, keeps the pot manageable, and plays well given our stack depth.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h8h. The Button raised to 250 and the Small Blind called. I need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 750. With 5:1 pot odds and a hand that plays reasonably well multi-way, calling is a very profitable and standard play.

On a flop of Jh 8s 2h, my pocket sevens are third pair. Being out of position against both the pre-flop raiser (Button) and the Big Blind, checking is the standard and safest play. Leading out here with a marginal made hand into two opponents is risky, as I'm beaten by any Jack or Eight, and checking allows me to evaluate the action from the other players.

I have middle pair (Eights) and a heart flush draw, which gives me significant equity on this board. In a three-way pot, checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) is the standard play. I want to see how the Button reacts before committing more chips. My hand is strong enough to call a bet or even check-raise if the situation warrants it.

I am in position as the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-high and a backdoor flush draw. The board is relatively dry (Jh-8s-2h). Both opponents checked to me, which often signals weakness or a willingness to play a check-call line. A continuation bet here is standard to maintain initiative and potentially win the pot immediately. I will bet 400 into the 750 pot, which is slightly more than half the pot, a good size to put pressure on my opponents.

On a flop of Jh 8s 2h, I hold pocket sevens, making it third pair with no strong redraws. Facing a continuation bet from the pre-flop raiser into two opponents, my hand is too weak to continue. Furthermore, I am out of position with the Big Blind still left to act behind me. Calling here would likely bleed chips, so folding is the standard and correct play.

I have middle pair (Eights) and a flush draw on a Jack-high board. Flashlight's 400 bet into 750 is a standard continuation bet. With 14 outs to likely improve to the best hand (9 hearts, 2 eights, 3 sixes) and some showdown value already, calling is the most appropriate action. It allows me to realize my equity and keep the pot size controlled while out of position.

On the turn, the board is Jh 8s 2h 3c. I have middle pair (Eights) and a heart flush draw. Pro (SB) has folded, so I am heads-up against Flashlight (BTN), the pre-flop aggressor. Since I am out of position, checking is the standard play to see how the aggressor proceeds. My hand has significant equity and some showdown value, making it a strong candidate to check-call and realize my equity on the river.

I hold Ace-high with no pair and no realistic draw on the current board of Jh-8s-2h-3c. Since the opponent called my continuation bet on the flop, their range is likely to include at least a pair or a reasonable draw, making them unlikely to fold to a second barrel. Betting again would turn my hand into a bluff with low equity. Checking behind is the optimal play to maintain pot control and take a free river card.

On the river, the board is Jh 8s 2h 3c Ac. I have middle pair (Eights). The heart flush draw missed. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, bet the flop but checked back the turn, which often indicates a hand with some showdown value that didn't want to build a larger pot, or a complete air/draw hand. The Ace on the river is an overcard that likely connects with Flashlight's range. Betting here doesn't achieve much as better hands (Aces, Jacks) won't fold and worse hands are unlikely to call. Checking is the most prudent action to reach showdown or evaluate if Flashlight bets.

The river brought an Ace, pairing my hole card and giving me top pair with an Ace kicker. The opponent checked to me, which indicates they likely have a weak made hand, such as a Jack or a smaller pair, or they missed their draws. My top pair is likely the best hand, and checking behind would result in a showdown, but betting allows me to extract value from their weaker holdings that might call a small-to-medium bet. I will bet 700 into the 1550 pot to gain value.

On the river, the board is Jh 8s 2h 3c Ac. I have a pair of Eights (third pair). Flashlight (the Button) checked back the turn after betting the flop. This turn check often signifies a marginal made hand (like a weak Jack or a pocket pair) or an Ace-high hand that wanted to see a cheap river. The Ace of clubs on the river is a significant card; it likely improves many hands in the Button's range that checked the turn (like Ax) to top pair. While Flashlight could be bluffing with missed heart draws, the Ace is a 'scare card' that makes it very difficult for my pair of Eights to be the best hand. Flashlight's bet of 700 into 1550 is large enough that I am not getting incredible odds to justify a hero call with third pair against a range that now contains many Aces. I will fold.
A7o is a standard opening hand from the button in 3-handed play. Raising to 2.5x to steal the blinds or play a pot in position with an Ace.

I am in the Small Blind holding 4d 2c, which is a very weak hand. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. Given the poor quality of my hand and the fact that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand, folding is the most prudent action to preserve my stack.

The opponent opened to 2.5 big blinds, which is a standard sizing from the button. Holding King-Eight offsuit, I have sufficient pot odds to defend my big blind. The cost of calling is 150 into a pot of 400, offering good enough returns to realize my equity post-flop, despite playing out of position.

The board is paired with low cards and a Jack, providing very little value for my King-high hand. As the out-of-position player, checking is the standard play to control the pot size and see if my opponent checks behind. I have no incentive to lead out into this dry board with nothing but high cards.

We raised preflop from the Button and the Big Blind called. The flop is 2d-2s-Jc, a very dry and disconnected paired board that favors the pre-flop raiser's range (we have all the overpairs and strong Jx). With Ace high, a continuation bet of around 1/3 pot is standard to fold out opponents' unmade hands (like KQ, QT, suited connectors) and deny their equity, while picking up the pot when they miss. I will c-bet roughly 1/3 to 2/5 of the pot.

I am holding King-High with no draw on a paired board of 2-2-J. My opponent has bet into me, and while the bet size is relatively small, I have no showdown value and no equity to improve. Continuing with this hand would require calling a bet with air, which is not a sustainable long-term strategy. I will fold and save my chips for a better spot.

Holding Ace-Five offsuit on the button in a three-handed game is a strong position. I will put in a standard opening raise of 2.5x the big blind to 250 chips. This allows me to take initiative and exert pressure on the blinds while playing a hand with reasonable equity and the benefit of position.

I am in the small blind with 8d-Tc, which is a weak hand. Facing a raise from the button, calling would put me in a disadvantaged position out of play against two other players. There is no incentive to defend this range here, so I will fold.

Holding A2o in the Big Blind facing a 2.5x raise from the Button is generally a fold. This hand has very poor playability post-flop, being offsuit, easily dominated by other Aces, and lacking connectivity. Calling out of position would often lead to difficult marginal situations, so I'll preserve my stack and look for better spots.

Holding 9-high offsuit (9h 4s) is a very weak hand with extremely low equity. In a 3-handed game, even from the button, this hand lacks the potential to be played profitably. Folding is the disciplined and optimal decision to minimize losses and preserve stack size for better opportunities.
96o is a very weak hand. Even against one opponent in the Big Blind, playing out of position with this hand is not profitable. Folding is the standard and best decision here.
Jack-Four offsuit is a weak hand and unplayable from the button in a standard 3-handed game. The correct play is to fold pre-flop and wait for a better starting hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Q4 offsuit. The button has folded, leaving just me and the Big Blind. While this is a blind-vs-blind situation, Q4o is a very weak hand to play out of position. The Big Blind (Flashlight) has shown aggression in previous hands and holds the chip lead. Folding is the most prudent move here to avoid playing a weak hand out of position.

I am on the button with 8s 6s, a solid speculative hand that plays well in position. In a three-handed game, opening with suited connectors from the button is standard to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. I'll raise to 2.5x the big blind.

The hand 7-5 offsuit is very weak and lacks potential for high-value showdowns. Defending from the Small Blind out of position against a button raise with such a poor hand results in a negative expected value. It is strategically sound to fold and preserve stack depth for stronger holdings.

Pocket jacks are a premium hand, significantly ahead of a standard Button opening range. 3-betting from the Big Blind builds the pot, extracts value, and denies equity to overcards. I will size up to 900 out of position to give the opponent bad odds to call with speculative hands.

I opened to 250 from the button with 8s 6s, and Pro 3-bet to 900 from the big blind. In a 3-handed game, 3-betting ranges are wider, and I have a speculative hand that plays very well in position. The price to call is 650 to win a pot of 1200, giving me roughly 2.8:1 odds. Given the stack depths and my positional advantage, calling to see a flop is the most profitable play, as I can potentially outplay the opponent post-flop or hit a strong draw/hand.

On a King-high flop with a flush draw, my pocket Jacks are reduced to a marginal bluff-catcher against a 3-bet calling range that contains many top pair combinations (AK, KQ, KJs) and strong draws. Betting here will mostly get called by better hands and strong draws, while folding out the worse hands I beat, like underpairs or missed broadways. Checking allows me to control the pot size and evaluate my opponent's action.

I have bottom two pair on a board that contains a King and a flush draw. My opponent 3-bet pre-flop and has now checked to me. While the King usually favors the 3-bettor's range, my two pair is extremely strong here. I need to bet for value and to protect against potential draws (like hearts or gutshots). Checking back would allow the opponent to realize equity for free. A bet of 1000 (roughly 54% of the pot) is large enough to get value from a King or a heart draw while keeping the pot manageable if I'm facing a trap.

On a K-6-8 board with two hearts, my pocket Jacks are reduced to a marginal bluff-catcher. Having the Jack of hearts blocks some of my opponent's potential flush draw bluffs (like QhJh or JhTh), which weights their betting range more heavily towards value hands like top pair (AK, KQ, KJs) or sets that called my pre-flop 3-bet. Facing a large bet out of position, I have very little equity against their value range and continuing would put me in extremely difficult situations on future streets. Folding is the most prudent choice to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 4s 5d. This is a very weak hand in a three-handed game. Even in position, playing unsuited low connectors like 4-5 offsuit is generally unprofitable long-term, as they perform poorly in multi-way pots and provide little strength for aggression. A disciplined approach at this stage of the tournament is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

King-Eight offsuit is a marginal holding to play out of position from the small blind. While it has some high-card value, playing it out of position against the Big Blind can lead to tricky post-flop spots, especially since Flash has shown aggression. I'll fold and look for a better spot.

Ah8h is a strong suited ace, especially when playing from the Button. It plays very well post-flop, making strong top pairs and nut flushes. I'll make a standard 2.5x open-raise to try and steal the blinds or build a pot where I will have positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. Q2o is a very weak hand and does not play well out of position, especially against a raise that is 2.5 times the big blind. Calling here would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with a weak range. Therefore, the standard and most profitable play is to fold.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a premium holding when defending the big blind against a button open. Given the pot odds and the relative stack sizes, calling is the most balanced and profitable approach. I have good equity and can navigate post-flop play effectively against Pro.

I have top two pair (Aces and Tens) on a coordinated board (Ace-Ten-Nine with a flush draw). This is a very strong hand that should lead out to build value and protect against potential draws. Betting 300 into the 550 pot is a balanced approach to extract value and charge any opponents holding flush or straight draws.

I have top pair with an eight kicker on a wet board (Ts Ac 9s). Flashlight donk bets slightly more than half-pot. This could be a weaker Ace, a draw (such as spades, or straight draws like QJ, KQ, J8), or a middle pair. Raising here would likely fold out his bluffs and weaker made hands while isolating me against hands that beat me (like two pair, sets, or better Aces). Calling is the best line to keep his range wide, control the size of the pot, and evaluate the turn card in position.

The turn card (8s) has made the board extremely coordinated, completing both potential straight draws (7-6, Q-J) and flush draws. While I have top two pair (Aces and Tens), my hand is now very vulnerable. Checking is the most prudent strategy here to maintain pot control, avoid bloating a pot when I might already be behind to a straight or a flush, and gauge my opponent's reaction to the new card.

The 8s on the turn improves me to two pair (Aces and Eights), but the board is extremely dangerous, completing both flush and straight draws. My opponent donk-bet the flop and checked the turn, which could indicate pot control with a weaker Ace, giving up on a bluff, or trapping with a completed flush/straight. If I bet, I risk folding out his weaker hands while getting called or check-raised by hands that beat me. Checking back exercises pot control, keeps his range wide, and gives me a free card to potentially improve to a full house.

The river pairing the Ten gives me a full house (Tens full of Aces) using my Ace of Spades and Ten of Hearts combined with the board. This is an extremely strong hand and likely the nuts, as no higher full house or quads are possible given the board texture. I need to extract value from Pro, who has shown passive play. A bet of 700 into the 1150 pot is a balanced size to encourage a call from hands like a flush or trip Aces while maximizing my return.

On this river, the paired Ten counterfeits my two pair (Aces and Eights), leaving me playing Aces and Tens with the 9 from the board as a kicker. I lose to any Ace with a Jack or better kicker, any Ten (trips), any flush, and any straight. The opponent donk-bet the flop, checked the turn when the flush and straight draws hit, and is now leading out on a very scary river. This line represents a lot of strength, likely a trapped flush, a straight, or trip Tens. Since I only beat pure bluffs and chop with weaker Aces, my hand is essentially a bluff catcher in a spot where bluffs are rare. Folding is the safest and most profitable play.

I am on the button with Ten-Nine of spades, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I will make a standard 3x open-raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the blinds.

I am in the small blind with Jack-Ten offsuit, facing a button raise from Flash. While JTo is slightly vulnerable, the pot odds are attractive enough to justify a call, allowing me to see a flop and evaluate the texture of the board. Given the stack depths, playing in position is not an option as the small blind, but defending is appropriate here given the relative hand strength.

We are in the Big Blind with 78s, a premium suited connector that plays exceptionally well in multi-way pots. Flash opened to 300 from the Button and Flashlight flat-called from the Small Blind. We are getting fantastic pot odds (calling 200 to win 700) and closing the action pre-flop. This is a perfect spot to flat call and see a flop, as our hand has great potential to flop strong draws, two pair, or trips. Squeezing is an option, but flatting keeps variance low and allows us to realize our equity smoothly.

I am sitting in the small blind with Jack-Ten offsuit on a flop of King-Queen-Seven. I have a gutshot straight draw to an Ace (Broadway), but my hand is essentially 'air' relative to the board, which heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range (containing many Kings and Queens). Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot size manageable and potentially see a free turn card if my opponents also check back. Betting into two opponents on this texture with a weak draw would be an unnecessary bluff that is unlikely to succeed.

We flopped bottom pair with a backdoor flush draw on a very wet, high-card heavy board (KQ7). The pre-flop raiser (Flash) is still to act behind us. This board heavily favors the ranges of our opponents, particularly the original raiser. Checking is the safest and most standard play here, allowing us to see what Flash does and potentially fold if the action gets heavy.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and both opponents have checked to me on a King-Queen high flop. This board likely hits some of their defending range, but consecutive checks from both players suggest they might be weak or playing cautiously. I will place a continuation bet of 450 (half the pot) to represent a strong hand like a King or Queen. This allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot right now, or at least narrow down their ranges if they decide to call.

I am facing a bet of 450 into a pot of 900. I hold a gutshot straight draw (needing an Ace) and two overcards (Jack and Ten) which have some potential against a King or Queen high board. Given the pot odds of 3:1, calling is the mathematically sound decision to realize my equity and see the turn card. Folding is too conservative given my draw, and raising would be turning a marginal hand into a bluff against a button aggressor who may be betting light.

With bottom pair and a backdoor flush draw on a K-Q-7 board, our hand is extremely weak, especially in a multi-way pot where both opponents have put chips in on the flop. Flash's c-bet and Flashlight's call strongly indicate that at least one of them has a King, Queen, or a strong draw like J-T. Our pair of sevens is heavily outmatched, and continuing here would be bleeding chips with terrible reverse implied odds.

The board paired the seven on the turn, which is a relatively neutral card, but my Jack-High hand remains weak. However, Flashlight has been quite aggressive in previous hands. Given the stack sizes and the 3-handed dynamic, I have the option to complete the small blind, raise, or fold. Completing allows me to see a flop cheaply in a pot that is already heads-up since the Button folded. Raising is also a viable option to try and take down the blinds, but J8o is a bit weak for a value raise. I will complete the bet to see a flop.

I am in the big blind with Ace-King offsuit, which is a very strong hand in this blind-versus-blind situation. The button has folded, and the small blind has limped. By raising to 300, I can isolate my opponent, take control of the hand, and build a pot with a significant equity advantage.

I completed from the Small Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit, and the Big Blind (Flashlight) responded with a raise to 300. While I am getting 2-to-1 odds to call, playing a mediocre hand like J8o out of position against an aggressive opponent like Flashlight is generally a losing strategy. Flashlight has shown significant aggression in previous hands, and I would rather fold now and wait for a better spot than commit more chips with a marginal hand while being out of position for the rest of the hand.

I am on the button with 9h 6c, which is a very weak starting hand. While the button is the best position to steal blinds, 9-6 offsuit is generally too weak to open-raise with, especially against opponents who have shown a willingness to defend or re-raise. Pro (Big Blind) has a shorter stack (65BB) and might be looking for a spot to shove or defend aggressively. Flashlight (Small Blind) is the chip leader and has been active. Given the 150-hand duration of the tournament and my healthy stack, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with junk hands. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the small blind with a very weak holding (9-2 offsuit). Despite the cheap price to complete the blind, playing such a weak hand out of position against the big blind is negative expected value. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in position on the button with 8d-9d, which is a strong hand for 3-handed play. Since no action has occurred yet, raising is the standard aggressive move to capitalize on my positional advantage and potential to win the blinds or take the initiative into the post-flop round.

J7 offsuit is a very weak hand, especially when facing a raise from the Button while being in the Small Blind. Playing out of position with a marginal holding will lead to negative expected value. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d 5c. Flashlight raised to 300 from the Button. Although I am getting 2:1 odds to call, this hand is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. There is no reason to defend such a marginal hand at this stage of the tournament.
K4o is a weak offsuit hand. Even on the button, it is not strong enough to open-raise or call, especially with both blinds still left to act. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 6c. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. 96s is a decent hand in a blind vs. blind scenario. While calling is an option, raising to 300 exerts pressure on the Big Blind and allows me to take control of the hand or win the pot immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with 5h-7s, a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand. While the pot odds offer a decent price to call (200 to win a pot of 400), playing this hand out of position against an aggressive Small Blind is suboptimal and likely to lead to difficult post-flop decisions. I prefer to fold and conserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with suited Queen-high. In a 3-handed game, this is a standard hand to open-raise with to put pressure on the blinds, especially since both Flashlight and Pro have shown a willingness to fold to pre-flop aggression in recent hands. A 3x raise is a standard sizing to attempt to take down the blinds or play a pot with initiative if called.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. Flash has raised from the Button to 300. With 50 already committed in the small blind, the call is 250. Given the stack sizes and the relative strength of A8o, calling to see a flop in position vs the button is a standard play to maintain pot control while seeing if I connect with the board.

I am in the Big Blind with JTo. The Button raised and the Small Blind called. While I get decent pot odds (200 to win 700), JTo is easily dominated by the ranges of both players and performs poorly out of position in a multi-way pot. Calling can lead to reverse implied odds, and squeezing with this specific combo isn't ideal due to lack of strong blockers. I will fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the small blind against the button. The board is K-K-5, which is extremely dry. My Ace-Eight offsuit has showdown value against total air but is behind almost any pocket pair or king in the button's range. Donking into the pre-flop raiser on this texture is generally poor strategy as it isolates me against the stronger part of their range. Checking is the standard play here to see what the button does; if they check back, I have successfully kept the pot small with a hand that has marginal showdown value.

On a dry, paired board like K-K-5, the pre-flop aggressor has a significant range advantage. Flashlight checked to me, which often indicates a weak hand or a desire to pot-control. My Queen-high has some showdown value but is vulnerable. A continuation bet of roughly 40% of the pot should be enough to take it down if they missed, which is likely for most of their calling range.

The board texture of King-King-Five is very dry and favors the Button's range, which likely contains many Kings or pocket pairs. My Ace-Eight offsuit has effectively zero showdown value against any of those hands, and with no draw to speak of, calling a continuation bet is just hoping my opponent is bluffing, which is statistically unlikely given the texture. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack and avoid playing a guessing game on later streets.

As the chip leader in the button position, I have a favorable spot to steal the blinds with King-Nine offsuit. Opening for 300 is a standard sizing that allows me to represent strength and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand in position.
I am in the Small Blind with J5o, a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, I have terrible position and poor equity. Folding is the only viable option.

I am in the Big Blind with 6-high offsuit (6c 4h). Flashlight (Button) has raised to 300. While the pot odds are roughly 3:1, my hand has very poor playability and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Given the strength of my hand relative to a typical button raising range, folding is the most prudent decision to conserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

With A4 suited on the Button, I have a strong enough hand to attempt a blind steal, and it plays well post-flop as it can make nut flushes and wheel straights. A standard open raise to 2.5x is appropriate here.

Holding Ten-Three offsuit in the small blind against a button open is an easy fold. The hand is very weak, lacks connectivity, and will be difficult to play out of position post-flop even if I were to hit a piece of the board.

I am in the Big Blind with Kc3c. The opponent on the button raised to 250. With 150 more to call into a pot of 400, I am getting roughly 2.6:1 pot odds. While K3s is a marginal hand, the price is good enough to defend, and the suitedness gives me some post-flop equity. I will call to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high and no pair or draw on a board of 9-4-6. The board does not provide much assistance to my hand, and playing out of position against the button, the most prudent action is to check and evaluate my opponent's next move.

I have bottom pair and a backdoor nut flush draw on a heavily coordinated board (9d 4h 6h). While a bet could deny equity to some overcards, it also exposes me to check-raises from numerous straight and flush draws in the Big Blind's range, and I will likely only get called by better hands or strong draws. Checking behind exercises pot control, allows me to realize my equity, and keeps the pot small with a marginal made hand.

I am acting first on the turn holding King-high with no pair or draw. The pot is relatively small, and my opponent checked behind on the flop, suggesting they likely have a marginal hand or are also checking down. Bluffing here would be risky and inefficient given my lack of equity, so checking is the most prudent course of action to maintain pot control and evaluate the river.

With the turn bringing a second 9, my pair of 4s gives me two pair (9s and 4s) with an Ace kicker. While I beat high-card hands and missed draws, I am behind any 6, pocket pairs like 55/77/88, or a trapped 9. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse made hands and might open me up to a check-raise from a strong draw or a slow-played hand. Checking again allows me to pot control, realize my showdown equity, and safely navigate to the river.

I am at the river holding King-high with no pair or draw. The board is paired (Nines) and the flush draw did not complete, though there are three hearts. My opponent, the button, checked the flop and turn, which indicates they might have a weak hand or a slow-played monster, but given the action, a bet here would be a pure bluff into a range that likely still contains Ace-high or pocket pairs that beat me. Checking is the standard play to reach showdown for free, as I cannot expect to win by betting.

On the river, the board completes a possible heart flush and pairs the 9, while putting a Ten out there. My pair of 4s is now very weak, equating to two pair (9s and 4s) with an Ace kicker. Since my opponent checked all three streets, they likely have a marginal made hand, Ace-high, or a missed draw. Betting here provides very little value because worse hands (like King-high or worse Ace-highs) will likely fold, while better hands (any Ten, Six, higher pocket pair, or flush) will call or even check-raise. Checking behind allows me to safely reach showdown and win against their missed draws or weaker Ace-highs.

I am on the button with 95s in a 3-handed game. This is a speculative hand, but it's strong enough to open for a raise to steal the blinds or play in position. Pro (BB) has a shorter stack and has been folding frequently to pre-flop aggression lately, and Flashlight (SB) has also shown a willingness to fold. A standard 3x open (300) is appropriate here to apply pressure and utilize my positional advantage.

I am in the small blind with J4s, which is a weak hand to defend against an open raise from the button. Being out of position for the rest of the hand makes this an unattractive call, especially with the big blind still left to act behind me. Folding is the most disciplined decision to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
K3o is too weak of a hand to defend from the Big Blind against a 3x Button open, especially out of position. Folding is the standard play to preserve the stack.

QTo is a solid holding in the button position, allowing me to take initiative and potentially steal the blinds or play a pot in position. A raise to 2.5x the big blind is a standard opening size for this stack depth.
I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak offsuit hand (2h 4s). Facing a raise from the Button, the correct and standard play is to fold, as this hand has practically no playability or equity.

I am holding 2c 6h in the big blind. Flashlight opened for 250 from the button, and Pro folded. While the pot odds are roughly 3.6-to-1 to call, my hand is extremely weak and plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against the chip leader. I will preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.
43o is a very weak starting hand. Being first to act pre-flop on the button in 3-handed play, folding is the standard and most profitable decision with this hand.

In a blind-vs-blind situation with Ten-Six offsuit, the hand is too weak to justify a raise against a chip leader who has shown a willingness to defend and play aggressively post-flop. While calling is an option to see a cheap flop, being out of position for the remainder of the hand with such a marginal holding is generally unprofitable. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 5d 3d, which is a marginal suited gapper. While opening from the button is generally a good strategy to exert pressure and take advantage of position, this hand is at the bottom of my opening range. Given the stack sizes and the fact that I want to maintain a solid image without over-committing to very weak holdings early in the tournament, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a stronger opening opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket Kings, which is a premium hand. Since the Button has folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. I need to build a pot and capitalize on my strong cards, so I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and hopefully extract value.

Jack-Three offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing a 3x open-raise from the Small Blind, the standard and most profitable play is to fold, as this hand lacks both high-card strength and playability post-flop.

With Jack-Nine offsuit in position on the button, I have the advantage to open the hand. Opening to 300 chips is a standard button raise to pressure the small and big blinds and take control of the pot.

Facing a 3x open from the Button, King-Nine offsuit is too weak to play from the Small Blind. Calling would put us out of position with a marginal, easily dominated hand, and 3-betting as a bluff with this specific combo is suboptimal compared to suited broadways or suited connectors. Folding is the standard, fundamentally sound play.

I am in the Big Blind with Qc 9d. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450. My pot odds are approximately 30.7%. Q9o is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind against a wide Button opening range in a 3-handed game. While out of position, the hand has enough high-card value and playability to see a flop rather than folding or turning it into a 3-bet bluff.

On a flop of As-7d-Qs, I have flopped middle pair (Queens) with a 9 kicker. While this is a decent hand in a 3-handed game, the presence of an Ace on the board is a significant threat given that Flashlight was the pre-flop raiser from the Button. Standard procedure as the defender in the Big Blind is to check to the aggressor to see their reaction. I'm looking to check-call a reasonable bet to see if they are continuation-betting with air or draws, or if they actually have the Ace.

The flop texture of Ace-Queen-Seven is extremely favorable for the big blind's calling range, as it contains high cards that typically hit a defender's range hard. With only Jack-high and no strong draw, a continuation bet would likely be called by any pair or better, making it a low-equity bluff. Checking behind is the optimal play to maintain pot control and see a free turn card, hoping to improve or reach a showdown more cheaply.

On the flop of As-7d-Qs, I checked my middle pair (Queens) and Flashlight, the pre-flop raiser, checked back. This check-back on an Ace-high board often indicates a lack of an Ace or a desire to pot control with a marginal hand. The turn 7h pairs the board. While it's possible Flashlight has a 7, it's statistically unlikely given their button opening range. I will check again to see if Flashlight attempts a delayed continuation bet or checks behind again, which would further confirm my hand is likely the best. I'm looking to get to a cheap showdown or induce a bluff from their air hands.

The turn card (7h) pairs the board, which usually slows down action. With only Jack-high, my hand has no showdown value against almost any calling range that defended the big blind, as those ranges are rich in Aces, Queens, and pocket pairs. Betting into the opponent would be a pure bluff, which is unlikely to force a fold from any reasonable hand. Checking behind is the optimal play to control the pot size and take a free river card, attempting to see a cheap showdown or hit a gutshot ten to improve to a straight.

The board has run out As-Qs-7d-7h-7s, giving everyone at least a full house, Sevens full of Aces (7-7-7-A-Q). My hole cards are Qc and 9d. My best five-card hand uses the three Sevens on the board, the Ace on the board, and the Queen in my hand (or on the board), resulting in Sevens full of Aces. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, checked both the flop and the turn, which strongly suggests they do not have an Ace. If they have an Ace, their hand would be Sevens full of Aces with an Ace kicker (7-7-7-A-A), which beats my hand. If they have a Queen or any other card lower than an Ace, we will chop the pot as we both play the 7-7-7-A-Q. Since I cannot beat an Ace and will likely chop with any other hand, there is no value in betting. Checking is the most logical move to reach a showdown.

I hold Jack-high on a board of A-Q-7-7-7. My opponent has checked every street, indicating they likely have a weak range but potentially enough showdown value to call a small bet, or they are trapping. Given that my Jack-high will lose to any Ace, Queen, or even a pocket pair, there is no value in betting as a bluff when the opponent has already shown reluctance to bet. Checking behind is the most conservative and correct play to conclude the hand with my weak holding.

Holding a medium-small pocket pair (5s 5h) on the Button, I have a clear opening hand. Opening gives me the chance to steal the blinds or play in position with a hand that can flop a well-hidden, strong set. A raise to 2.5x (250) is standard and risks a relatively small amount to win the current pot.

Ace-Jack offsuit is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Facing a button raise, I should 3-bet for value and to gain initiative, especially since I'll be out of position post-flop. A raise to 850 (roughly 3.4x the open) is a standard sizing to put pressure on the opener and discourage the Big Blind from entering the pot lightly.

7h 8s is a very weak hand to defend with, especially against a 3-bet from the small blind while I am out of position in the big blind. Calling a 3-bet with such a marginal holding is generally unprofitable. I will fold and save my stack for a better opportunity.

We are facing a fairly large 3-bet from the Small Blind. We have pocket fives, a hand that relies almost entirely on hitting a set to win a large pot. To profitably set-mine, we generally need our opponent's remaining stack (and our own) to be at least 10-15 times the amount we have to call. We have to call 600, and we only have about 5,375 chips behind. This gives us less than 10-to-1 implied odds, making it mathematically unprofitable to call purely for set value. Since we will miss the flop 88% of the time and be forced to fold to a continuation bet, the most prudent play is to fold and preserve our stack.

On the button with 86s, I have a standard opening hand in a 3-handed game. My stack is healthy at 112bb. A raise to 3x the big blind (300) is a consistent play that applies pressure on the blinds while allowing me to play a pot in position if called. If I encounter a significant re-raise, I can re-evaluate, but 86s has enough playability to justify an open here.

I am in the Small Blind with K3o, which is a weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive button raiser (Flash). Calling here would commit 250 chips into a pot where I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, likely resulting in a difficult post-flop scenario. Given the hand strength and positional disadvantage, folding is the most disciplined and profitable play.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand, Queen-Three offsuit. The Button raised to 300, which is 3 big blinds. My hand does not have good playability or equity against a standard Button opening range, so folding is the optimal and standard play here.

My hand is [3h, 8c], which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Regardless of my position or the table dynamics, playing this hand is -EV. I should fold and wait for a more playable starting hand to continue protecting my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit, one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Being out of position against the Big Blind with such a poor hand, the optimal play is to fold and save my chips for a better spot.

J6s is slightly too weak to open from the Button even in 3-handed play. With around 60 big blinds, we can afford to wait for a better hand to enter the pot and avoid playing a marginal holding that could face a 3-bet from the larger stacks.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo). Since the Button has folded, I am in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. ATo is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind scenario, significantly ahead of the random range the Big Blind might hold. To exert pressure and build the pot with my equity advantage, I will raise to 300, which is consistent with the standard raise sizing observed in previous hands.

Defending the big blind against a small blind raise with Q7o. Although the hand is marginal and I will be out of position post-flop, the pot odds (200 to call for a 400 pot) are favorable enough to justify a call, especially considering the wide opening range of the small blind in heads-up scenarios.

I was the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-Ten offsuit, and the flop comes King-Eight-Eight. This is a very favorable board texture for a continuation bet because it is relatively dry and favors my perceived range as the raiser. While I currently only have Ace-high, a bet here allows me to take down the pot immediately if the Big Blind missed the board, which is likely. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to maintain pressure and represent a strong hand like a King or a pocket pair.

The board of 8-8-K is extremely dry and unfavorable for my hand of Queen-high. I have no pair, no draws, and essentially zero equity against the range that the small blind would use to bet on this texture. Continuing with a call here would be a purely speculative bluff-catch that is unlikely to be profitable, so folding is the correct disciplined decision.

I am on the button with suited connectors (3s 4s). In a three-handed game, the button should open a wide range of hands to apply pressure on the blinds and take advantage of positional advantage for the rest of the hand. While 3s 4s is at the bottom of my opening range, its suited connectivity provides some post-flop playability. I will make a standard raise to 300.

Holding 9h 6d in the small blind against a button open is a clear fold. The hand has poor equity and I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand. It is better to conserve my stack for a more advantageous spot.

With King-Queen offsuit in the Big Blind facing a Button open, I have a very strong hand against the Button's wide raising range. A 3-bet is a strong play to extract value, take the betting initiative, and deny equity. I'll size my 3-bet to 900 (3x the open) to put pressure on my opponent while keeping the pot manageable if called.

I opened from the button with 3s 4s, which is at the very bottom of my opening range. The Big Blind (Pro) has 3-bet to 900. While I have the advantage of position, my hand has very low equity against a standard 3-betting range and does not realize its equity well in 3-bet pots. Pro has a relatively short stack (around 52 BB), making the potential for deep-stack maneuvering limited if I call. Given the strength of the 3-bet and the weakness of my holding, folding is the most disciplined and stack-preserving play here.

My hand, 7s2s, is extremely weak and has very poor playability. Even in a three-handed game on the button, there is no strategic justification for playing such a hand. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play to conserve my stack and wait for a more favorable opportunity.

With A7o in the small blind and the action folded to me, it is a great spot to attack the big blind. Raising to 3x (300) puts pressure on Flash and allows me to either win the blinds uncontested or take the betting lead into the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. With a stack of over 11,000 chips, calling 200 more into a pot that will be 600 total gives me 3:1 odds. Ten-Nine offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend in a blind-versus-blind battle, as it has decent equity against a wide Small Blind opening range and plays well enough post-flop to see a board.

With Ace-high on a relatively dry, rainbow Jack-high board, I missed the flop completely and lack any strong backdoor draws. While I have the pre-flop range advantage, c-betting this specific holding isn't mandatory, as my Ace-high occasionally retains marginal showdown value against floats. Checking allows me to control the pot size and evaluate Flash's response.

I have an open-ended straight draw (9-10 on a J-8-3 board). The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has checked, showing potential weakness or a desire to pot control. I will bet 300 (half-pot) as a semi-bluff. This action puts pressure on Pro's non-pairing hands like AK/AQ and allows me to take down the pot now, while retaining about 32% equity to hit my straight by the river if called.

I missed the flop completely with A7o, holding only Ace-high with no straight or flush draws. Playing out of position against a half-pot bet from the big blind is a losing proposition when I have virtually no equity and no good way to improve. Continuing would simply be bleeding chips, so the most sensible play is to fold and wait for a better spot.
5s 2s is a very weak, disconnected hand. Even on the button, this is a clear fold pre-flop.

With the button folding, I am in a blind-vs-blind situation against Flashlight. Jh 8d is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in this spot to apply pressure and potentially win the pot pre-flop. I will raise to 300, which is a standard 3x open to take the initiative.

Flash raised to 300 from the small blind. With Kh-6h in the big blind, I am getting 2:1 pot odds on the call, which makes defending my big blind a profitable play. I will call the 200 chips to see a flop and re-evaluate.

I opened Jh 8d from the small blind and Flashlight called from the big blind. The flop is Ks 2d 6s. This flop hits an opening range better than a defending range generally, but with a King on the board, it favors the pre-flop aggressor. This is a standard continuation bet on a dry-ish, high-card board.

I have flopped two pair (Kings and Sixes) on a relatively dry board (Ks-2d-6s). Flash has been aggressive, and this board hits a wide range of his opening hands. With two pair, I am currently ahead of his continuation betting range, which likely consists of Kings with weaker kickers, mid-pocket pairs, or semi-bluffs involving spade draws. Raising to 900 allows me to build a pot, protect against potential flush or straight draws, and extract value from worse Kings.

Flashlight has raised my continuation bet on a King-high board. My hand, Jack high with no draws, has almost no equity against a range that includes Kings, sets, or even flush draws. In a 3-handed game, the button should be opening a wide range of hands to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. I am in the small blind with Queen-Six offsuit, which is a weak hand to play out of position against a button raise. Flash has been opening frequently, but Q6o has poor equity and playability post-flop. Calling would be marginal and likely lead to difficult spots, so folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand, 8c 2c. Facing a button raise from Flash, calling or 3-betting with such a low equity and disconnected hand is unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fives, which is a strong starting hand in this position. Since the pot has not yet been opened, I will open-raise to 250 to isolate the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or gain the initiative in a favorable position.
J5o is a trash hand, especially out of position against a button raise. An easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with QJs, a strong broadway hand that performs well in multi-way pots or heads-up. Flashlight (Button) has raised to 250. While I could 3-bet, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has good playability without bloating the pot out of position against a stack that currently covers me. On this paired board of 3c-3h-8c, I have two overcards with QJ. As the caller out of position, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most prudent play. This allows me to see how Flashlight reacts. Given the dry nature of the board, they may check back with a variety of hands, or I can evaluate their bet size to see if it's worth floating with my overcards.

The board is very dry (3-3-8), which hits my pocket fives well. Since the opponent checked, I have the initiative and should bet to capitalize on my equity, force folds from overcards, and extract value from any weaker pairs.

Flashlight's bet of 300 into a 550 pot is a standard continuation bet on a paired, relatively dry board. With two overcards (Queen and Jack), I have significant equity against a wide button opening range. Calling 300 to win a total pot of 850 (approx 26% equity needed) is justified as I can improve to a strong top pair on many turn cards, and my current high-card value may still be ahead of some of Flashlight's air bluffs.

On the turn, the board has updated to 3c-3h-8c-2h, which is quite dry and doesn't change the board texture significantly. As the big blind who called the flop bet, checking to the original aggressor remains the most standard and balanced play. I currently hold Queen-Jack high, which still has some showdown value against complete air and can improve to the best hand on the river if a Queen or Jack appears. I will check to see how Flashlight proceeds before deciding whether to continue.

The board texture is 3-3-8-2 and my pocket fives are a marginal pair. The opponent checked the turn, which may indicate a lack of strength or an intention to induce. Betting now would likely only be called by better hands (any eight, any three, or overpairs) or might force out hands I am currently beating. Checking back provides a free card to the river, controls the pot size, and preserves my showdown value without overcommitting to a vulnerable hand.

On the river, the Ace of Diamonds is a significant card that likely improves the range of the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight). After Flashlight checked the turn, it's possible they were pot-controlling a marginal hand or even an Ace. My Queen-Jack high has zero showdown value, but attempting to bluff at this pot into a range that contains many Aces (which Flashlight would have opened from the Button) is risky. I will check and concede the pot unless Flashlight also checks and somehow has a weaker high-card hand, which is unlikely.
