A 3-bet to 900 is a standard sizing to take control of the hand, put pressure on the opponent, and leverage my stack advantage. Calling is also an option, but 3-betting allows me to potentially take down the pot pre-flop or define the opponent's range while holding the initiative.

I opened with a raise to 300 from the Small Blind with 8h7h, a strong speculative hand. Flashlight, in the Big Blind, has 3-bet to 900. While 8h7h has great playability, I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Calling requires investing 600 more into a 1200 pot, and against a likely strong 3-betting range from an active opponent, the implied odds are offset by the difficulty of playing this hand out of position. Given the stack depths and the positional disadvantage, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 8h4h. While this is a suited hand, it is quite weak (8-high, two-gapper). Looking at the table dynamics, Flashlight (SB) has recently shown aggression by 3-betting my button opens (Hand #74), and Pro (BB) is at a stack size (approx. 53bb) where they can profitably defend or shove over a wide opening range. Opening 84s here is likely a bit too loose against these opponents. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (4c 8d). Given the low value of the cards, there is no strategic advantage to calling to see a flop out of position. It is better to fold and preserve my stack for a stronger starting hand.

I am on the button with a very weak hand, Jc-4d. With 150 hands to play in this tournament and a comfortable stack lead, there is no reason to open-raise with such a marginal holding. It is better to conserve chips and wait for a more premium starting hand.

I have 8-3 offsuit in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand. The Button folded, leaving just me and the Big Blind. Even though it's a blind vs. blind situation, 83o is too weak to limp or raise profitably, especially being out of position. The best action is to fold and preserve my chips for better spots.
Q2 offsuit is a very weak hand. Even on the button, it is a clear fold. I want to wait for better opportunities to play and maximize my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 2s 4s, which is a very weak hand even in 3-handed play. The Button has folded, leaving me heads-up against Flashlight in the Big Blind. While I only need to commit 50 more chips to see a flop, 2s 4s has poor equity and post-flop playability, especially out of position. Previous hands (like Hand #38) show that Flashlight is willing to raise against Small Blind completions. Rather than calling and potentially facing a raise or playing a weak hand out of position, I will choose to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with King-Deuce suited in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening range hand from this position. Raising allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately, or play the hand in position post-flop if called. Given the stack sizes, a standard raise to 300 is appropriate.

KJo is a solid starting hand for defending the small blind against a button open. Given my stack size and the pot odds, calling is the most balanced approach to realize my equity and evaluate the flop. I will call the raise of 250, making my total contribution to the pot 300.

With pocket sevens in the Big Blind facing a button raise and a small blind call, we are getting excellent pot odds to set-mine. Squeezing here could be viable, but with 77, playing a multi-way pot and trying to flop a set is a very profitable and low-variance line, especially given my stack size relative to the pot.

I have top pair with a King kicker on a board of Q-K-8 with a spade draw. While this is a strong hand, leading out into two opponents on a wet board can be risky and unnecessarily bloat the pot. Checking allows me to maintain pot control, gauge the strength of my opponents' hands based on their actions, and re-evaluate on the turn. This is the most conservative and strategic approach for this position.

The board comes Qd 8s Ks, featuring two overcards to my pocket sevens and a flush draw. This texture connects heavily with the ranges of both the pre-flop raiser and the caller. Out of position, betting here is not profitable. The standard play is to check and evaluate, leaning heavily towards folding if there is any significant action.

On this flop of Qd-8s-Ks, I have top pair with a King. Although my kicker (Deuce) is weak, in a three-way pot after a pre-flop raise, checking back would be too passive and risks giving free cards to straight or flush draws (like J-T or spade draws). I'll lead with a bet to extract value from weaker Kings, Queens, or draws and to protect my hand. A bet of around half the pot (450) is standard here to define where I am and build the pot.

I have top pair with a King and a Jack kicker on a board of Q-K-8 with a spade draw. While my hand is decent, the board is coordinated enough that leading or raising could be dangerous and potentially bloat the pot against stronger hands like AK or KQ. Calling the bet of 450 is the most measured approach, allowing me to maintain pot control and evaluate the turn card while keeping my equity in the hand against potential bluffs or weaker draws.

The board is Qd 8s Ks. My 7s 7h are an underpair to the three board cards. Flash has bet 450 and Flashlight has called. Given the heavily coordinated board with multiple overcards and flush draws, my pocket sevens have almost zero equity against two opponents who are showing interest. Folding is the only sensible option.

I have flopped trips with Kings on a coordinated board of Q-8-K-K. While my hand is very strong, the board has some connectivity and the King on the turn makes it slightly more likely that an opponent could have a full house (if they held QQ or 88, though those are limited). Given that I am out of position, checking to my opponent, who has been aggressive, is a solid strategic move. It allows me to trap them if they have a weaker hand or a draw and want to bluff, while also keeping the pot controlled if they happen to have a monster hand. This approach maximizes my potential value while minimizing unnecessary risk.

On the turn, the second King significantly strengthens my hand, giving me trips. Given that I was the pre-flop aggressor and bet the flop, and Flashlight called the flop bet before checking the turn, it is highly likely I have the best hand. Flashlight could be holding a Queen, a pocket pair, or a draw (like a straight draw with JT or a spade flush draw). I will bet for value to extract more chips from a Queen or to charge the draws. A bet of 900 (half the pot) is a standard value sizing here that keeps their range wide enough to call.

I have flopped trip Kings (Kh, Ks, Kd) and am facing a bet of 900 on the turn. My hand is extremely strong, and I am clearly ahead of most of Flash's range, which likely consists of top pairs with good kickers, two pairs, or straight draws. Raising now allows me to extract maximum value from hands like KQ or QJ that might be betting for value, and protects against the board potentially becoming more dangerous on the river. A raise to 1900 puts appropriate pressure on my opponent and builds a larger pot for the river.

On the turn, I have improved to three of a kind (Kings). However, my kicker (Deuce) is very weak. Flashlight's check-raise to 1900 is a significant display of strength, typically indicating a stronger King (like K-J or K-A) or potentially a full house (8-8). However, the raise is a minimum raise (only 1000 more to call), giving me pot odds of 4.6-to-1. At these odds, folding trips is too conservative, as they could be semi-bluffing with a straight draw (J-T) or a flush draw, or simply overvaluing a Queen. I will call to see the river and evaluate based on the final card and their subsequent action, but I must be wary of being out-kicked.

I have flopped trip Kings and the board has paired twice (Queens and Kings), giving me Kings full of Queens. This is an extremely strong hand that only loses to exactly Quad Queens. Given the aggressive action on the turn, it is highly likely the opponent has a strong hand like a Queen, an Ace, or a smaller full house that would call a value bet. I will bet 1500 to extract maximum value from these second-best hands, while still maintaining a reasonable bet size that won't alienate calling ranges.

On the river, the board is [Qd, 8s, Ks, Kd, Qh]. My hand [Kc, 2c] gives me a Full House, Kings full of Queens (K-K-K-Q-Q). Looking at the rankings: 1) Pocket Queens would have Four of a Kind (Q-Q-Q-Q-K), which is extremely unlikely given the betting history. 2) Any King (like AK, KJ, or my K2) makes the same Full House (K-K-K-Q-Q) because the two Queens on the board are higher than any other card besides a King, resulting in a split pot. 3) Any Queen (like AQ or QJ) makes Queens full of Kings (Q-Q-Q-K-K), which is lower than my Kings full. 4) A hand like 8-8 makes 8-8-8-K-K, also lower than mine. Flashlight's check-raise on the turn and lead on the river strongly suggests a King. Since any King results in a split pot and raising only risks losing more to the virtually impossible pocket Queens or having a King call to split anyway, calling is the most standard and safe play to secure my half of the large pot.

I am on the button with a strong offsuit hand, KQo. As the chip leader, I want to apply pressure and build a pot or take down the blinds. An opening raise is standard here to exercise positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with 2c 3h, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button, I have no reason to play this hand, especially out of position. Folding is the only viable option.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Nine offsuit. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind has folded. The pot currently stands at 450, and it costs me 200 to call. This gives me pot odds of 2.25:1. King-Nine offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard Button opening range, especially given our deep stacks. I will call to see the flop rather than raising, as K9o performs well in a defended pot but can be difficult to play against a 4-bet.

On a flop of 9s-7d-Th, I have middle pair (Nines) with a King kicker. I am out of position against Flashlight, who was the pre-flop aggressor from the button. Checking to the aggressor is the standard and most prudent play here with a medium-strength hand. This allows me to see how Flashlight reacts to the board texture, which contains several straight draws and one overcard to my pair. If he bets, I plan to call to evaluate the turn, as my hand is too strong to fold to a single continuation bet but not strong enough to lead out into the aggressor.

The board is coordinated (9-7-T), and although I have overcards and a gutshot straight draw, I have missed the flop and have limited showdown value. Checking back is the standard play here to maintain pot control, realize equity, and see a free turn card rather than bloating the pot or risking a check-raise.

On the turn 6c, the board is now 9s-7d-Th-6c. I have middle pair with a King kicker. Flashlight checked back the flop, which often suggests a marginal hand or a surrender on a coordinated board. By checking again, I am continuing to play my hand as a medium-strength showdown hand. Leading here might force out weaker hands that I beat while getting called by hands that have me beat (like a Ten or a straight). Checking allows me to see if Flashlight will bet with his draws or air, or simply check behind again to get to a cheap showdown. If he bets, I have a clear call given the pot odds and the likelihood that my Nines are still good.

On the turn, the board is [9s, 7d, Th, 6c]. With my hand [Qh, Ks], I have an open-ended straight draw (any Jack or 8 gives me a straight) along with two overcards. While checking back is a safe option, betting allows me to exert pressure on the opponent's checking range and potentially take down the pot immediately, or build value if I hit my straight on the river. Betting roughly 60% of the pot (400 into 650) is an appropriate size to achieve fold equity while maintaining a good price to realize my draw.

I have middle pair (Nines) with a King kicker on a board of 9-7-T-6. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor from the button, checked the flop, which often suggests he doesn't have a Ten or better, or is playing a marginal hand passively. His turn bet of 400 into 650 could be a delayed continuation bet, a semi-bluff with a straight draw (like 8x or Jx), or a value bet with a weaker Nine. Given my hand strength and the pot odds (calling 400 to win 1050), folding is too tight and raising doesn't accomplish much. I will call to evaluate the river.

I have a pair of Nines with a King kicker. The board is quite coordinated with potential straights (J8, 85) and overcards (Ten, Queen). Flashlight checked the flop and bet the turn, which could indicate a Ten, an Eight, or a complete air bluff. The river Queen is a scare card that likely improves some of the Button's range (AQ, KQ, QJ, QT). There is no value in betting here as I likely only get called by better hands. Checking is the best option to either see a cheap showdown or potentially induce a bluff from missed draws like AJ or KJ.

I have top pair with Qh Ks on a 9-7-T-6-Q board. My opponent checked the river. While I could try for a thin value bet against a Ten, the board texture is wet with multiple straight possibilities (any 8 makes a straight). Checking behind is the prudent choice to take my showdown value and avoid getting check-raised by a hand that beat me or getting folded out by a bluff.
Pre-flop, 3s 6c offsuit from the Button is an incredibly weak hand. The best play is an easy fold to conserve chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket nines, which is an extremely strong hand in a blind-versus-blind situation. Since the button folded, I will raise to build the pot and take the initiative against the Big Blind. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

Flash raised to 300 from the small blind, and I am in the Big Blind. With Q6 offsuit, I have reasonable pot odds to defend, although the hand strength is marginal. Calling allows me to see the flop in position against an aggressive opponent. I will call the 200 chips required to match the raise, bringing my total contribution to this street to 300.

On a flop of Kh-As-5c, my pocket nines have been significantly devalued by the presence of two overcards. Pre-flop, I raised from the Small Blind and was called by the Big Blind. This board texture heavily favors the Big Blind's calling range, which contains many Ax and Kx hands. Since I am out of position and the board is quite 'dry' for my specific hand, checking is the best play to control the pot and see how the opponent reacts. Betting here often only gets called by better hands and forces folds from hands I already beat.

I am in the big blind with Queen-high on an Ace-high, King-high board. My hand has no showdown value and no significant draws. Since Flash checked, checking behind is the best decision to control the pot size and see the turn card for free.

On a turn of 2s following a check-check on the Kh-As-5c flop, the board state remains largely unchanged. My pocket nines are likely either way ahead of a missed hand or way behind a slow-played Ace or King. By checking again, I am exercising pot control and keeping the pot small with a hand that has showdown value but cannot comfortably bet for multiple streets of value on such a high-card board. If Flashlight checks back, it reinforces the likelihood that my nines are the best hand. If Flashlight bets, I will have to evaluate the bet sizing to decide on a call.

Flash checked the turn on a board of Kh As 5c 2s. Holding Qs 6d, I have no showdown value, no pair, and no draw. Betting into the opponent would effectively turn a worthless hand into a bluff against a range that might contain an Ace or King. Checking behind is the optimal play to maintain pot control and see the river for free.

On a river board of Kh-As-5c-2s-3h, my pocket nines have significant showdown value but little value as a bet. The action has been checked through on the flop and turn, suggesting my opponent likely does not have a strong Ace or King, but they could easily have a small piece or a straight with any 4. Betting here would likely only result in being called by hands that beat me (like a slow-played Ace, King, or a straight) while forcing folds from hands I already beat. Checking is the most prudent play to realize my equity and win the pot at showdown.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-high on an Ace-King-5-2-3 board. The hand has been checked down by both players through the turn and river. Since I have no showdown value and Flash is a pre-flop aggressor who likely has at least a pair if they hit any part of this board, betting would only be successful as a pure bluff. Given the passive nature of the hand so far, checking behind is the optimal play to avoid unnecessary losses against a potentially stronger range that isn't folding to a small bet.

I am on the button with 8c 7d, which is a decent hand for a steal attempt in a 3-handed game. My stack is healthy, and a standard 3x open (300 chips) has been my consistent sizing for opening the pot. If the blinds show resistance, I can easily fold this marginal hand, but in position, it's worth trying to take down the blinds or play a pot with initiative.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c-2d, which is a very weak hand. Being out of position against an aggressive button raiser makes it even worse. There is no strategic value in defending this hand, so folding is the correct, disciplined decision to preserve my stack.

Facing a 3bb open from the Button while in the Big Blind with about 50 big blinds. Ten-Seven suited is a marginal suited two-gapper that plays relatively poorly out of position against a full 3x raise. To preserve our stack and avoid difficult, marginal spots post-flop, folding is the most fundamentally sound decision here.

With Queen-Ten offsuit on the button, I have a standard opening hand. In a three-handed game, the button should open frequently to steal the blinds and take the initiative. A raise to 300 is consistent with my previous betting patterns and puts sufficient pressure on the blinds.

With pocket sixes in the small blind facing a standard button open, set-mining is the optimal strategy. Our effective stack is roughly 4800, which gives us around 19:1 implied odds—well above the typical 15:1 needed to profitably call and set-mine out of position. I'll flat call to keep the pot manageable and try to spike a set on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Deuce offsuit. The button (Flashlight) raised to 300, and the Small Blind (Pro) called. I need to put in 200 more chips to call into a pot that will be 900. These are excellent pot odds (3.5 to 1), and since I am closing the pre-flop action, I will see the flop for a relatively small investment despite the hand's weakness and being out of position.

With pocket sixes on a 3-3-2 flop, I have an overpair to the board, but the pot is 3-way and I'm out of position. Checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard play here. This board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's c-betting range, and by checking, I can control the size of the pot and evaluate the actions of both the Big Blind and the Button before deciding how to proceed.

I have bottom pair on a paired board (3s and 2s). While this is a decent hand on such a dry board, I am in a 3-way pot against the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) who has yet to act on this street. Checking is the standard play here to see how the button reacts and to keep the pot size manageable with a marginal hand.

The board is extremely dry with paired treys and a deuce. As the pre-flop aggressor holding Queen-high, I have very little equity. Betting here would be a pure bluff, and since my opponents have checked, they may be trapping or just checking down. Checking back allows me to maintain control of the pot size, take a free card to see if I improve, and avoid unnecessarily bloating the pot with a weak hand.

After the flop checks through, my pocket sixes are very likely the best hand. The turn brings a 5, giving me an open-ended straight draw (if a 4 comes) to go with my overpair to the board (excluding the paired 3s). Betting here for value and protection against overcards and flush draws is the optimal approach. A bet of slightly less than half the pot should accomplish this while keeping the pot manageable.

On a paired board of [3s 3c 2s 5h], I have bottom pair with a King kicker. After the flop checked through among all three players, the Small Blind (Pro) led out for 400 into a 900 pot on the turn. This lead often represents a marginal hand like a 5, a small pocket pair, or a complete bluff attempting to take down a 'dead' pot. Given the pot odds (3.25 to 1) and the fact that my pair of 2s beats all pure bluffs and some of the turn-stabbing range, I have a profitable call to see the river. While Flashlight is still behind me, their flop check suggests they are unlikely to have a monster or a high pocket pair, reducing the risk of a raise.

The board (3s-3c-2s-5h) is very low, and both opponents have shown strength on the turn with a bet and a call. My Queen-high hand has no showdown value and no realistic way to improve to a winning hand against two opponents who are already committed to the pot. Calling would be an inefficient use of my stack, as the pot odds do not outweigh the low probability of having the best hand. The most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

On the river, the 9c completes no obvious draws but is an overcard to my pair of sixes. The missed spade flush draw is a common holding in the Big Blind's calling range, but there are also hands like 77, 88, A4, or a rivered 9x that now beat me. Betting here would likely only get called by better hands and fold out the missed draws I currently beat. Therefore, checking to realize showdown value and potentially induce a bluff from a missed flush draw is the optimal play.

On a board of 3s 3c 2s 5h 9c, I hold bottom pair (2s) with a King kicker. Pro led the turn for 400 after the flop checked around, which usually represents a marginal hand like a 5, a small pocket pair, or a bluff. Pro's check on the river suggests they are either giving up on a bluff or looking to get to a cheap showdown with a hand like a 5 or an Ace-high. Since my pair of 2s is weak and unlikely to get a better hand to fold or a worse hand to call, checking back to realize my showdown value is the most logical and safest play.

J3o is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Even from the Button, it falls well outside of any standard or profitable opening range. With two larger stacks in the blinds, attempting to steal here is unnecessarily risky. The best play is to simply fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

Q2o is a very weak hand to play from the small blind, even with the button folding. While it is only 50 more to complete, the hand lacks connectivity and is easily dominated by the big blind's range. Playing out of position against Flashlight, who is the chip leader, with such a marginal hand is an unnecessary risk. Folding is the most disciplined play to maintain stack integrity.

I am on the button with 7c6c, which is a strong suited connector. Opening from the button with this hand is standard to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds. A 3x raise to 300 has been my consistent sizing for opening raises throughout the tournament and should work well here to either take the pot or set up a favorable post-flop situation.

Holding 9h 5h in the small blind against a button open is a marginal spot at best. The hand has poor equity and I will be out of position for the entire hand. Given the tournament stage and my current stack, it is better to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation rather than committing chips to a weak holding.

J3o is a very weak unsuited hand. Facing a raise from the button, there is no justification for defending the Big Blind with this holding. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with a suited hand (Qd 9d), which is a strong enough starting hand to open from this position. Stealing the blinds is a priority to maintain my stack lead and exercise my positional advantage. A raise to 250 is a standard sizing in this tournament structure to put pressure on the blinds.

Holding 9c 2h in the small blind facing a raise from the button. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em, making it a mandatory fold. I will easily release this hand and wait for a better spot to invest my chips.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket sixes. The Button has opened with a standard 2.5x raise. In this spot, I have the choice between calling to set-mine or three-betting to take initiative and exert pressure on a likely wide button opening range. Given my previous history of three-betting to 850 over 250-chip opens (Hands #32, #50, #62, #65), I will maintain this sizing to remain consistent and polarized. Pocket sixes are strong enough to defend the blind aggressively here, and a raise may end the hand immediately or allow me to lead on favorable low-card boards.

I opened the pot with Q9s from the button, a reasonable hand to steal the blinds. Flash 3-bet to 850. Getting approximately 2:1 on a call (600 to win a total pot of 1800), and being in position with a suited connector, it is mathematically correct to call and see a flop. Folding would be too weak given the pot odds and position, and 4-betting with Q9s is unnecessary variance. I will call the 600 to see the flop.

I have flopped a set of sixes on a 7h-Jh-6c board. This is a very strong hand, but the board is somewhat coordinated with straight and flush draws. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor (3-betting to 850), a continuation bet is standard to maintain initiative and extract value from Jacks, straight draws, and heart draws. I will bet 900 into the 1750 pot to build the pot size and charge any draws while still being a size that top-pair hands will likely call.

Flash bet 900 into a pot of 1700 on a board of 7h-Jh-6c. My hand (Qd-9d) lacks any significant equity (no flush draw, no strong straight draw, only two overcards) and is currently losing to almost any hand in the opponent's range. Given the lack of pot odds or future potential, calling is not mathematically sound. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.
J4o is a very weak hand. As the first to act pre-flop, I should easily fold this garbage hand.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Ten offsuit. The Button has folded, leaving just me and the Big Blind. K10o is a very strong hand for a head-up situation against the Big Blind's random range. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to take control of the pot and apply pressure.

Flash raised from the small blind to 300. I am in the big blind with King-Nine offsuit. This is a classic blind-vs-blind defensive spot. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 400), defending by calling is the most sound and balanced play. I have a reasonably strong hand in position-relative terms for this specific matchup, and I want to see a flop rather than inflating the pot out of position against an aggressive opponent.

I have top pair with a King kicker on a relatively dry flop. I raised pre-flop and was called by the Big Blind. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot, to get value from weaker tens, draws, or middle/bottom pairs, and to protect my hand from overcards or potential diamond draws.

The board (3h 4d Td) is relatively dry and favors neither player exclusively, though the Ten and Diamond give it some texture. Flash is betting half-pot (300 into 600), which is a common continuation bet sizing with a wide range from the small blind. With King-high, I have two overcards and reasonable equity against a range of underpairs or air. Folding would be too passive given the pot odds of 3:1, and raising would likely fold out the hands I am currently beating while isolating myself against stronger holdings. Calling allows me to keep the pot controlled and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have top pair with a King kicker (Ks Tc) on a board of 3h-4d-Td-6s. The turn 6s is a brick that doesn't change much. I led the flop and was called by Flashlight. I need to continue betting for value against weaker Tens, middle pairs, and to charge any diamond flush draws. I will bet 600, which is half the pot.

Given the pot odds (3:1) and my read of Flash's aggressive but potentially wide betting range, calling the 600 bet on this turn is the most rational approach. My King-high has some equity, and I want to avoid folding to potential bluffs while keeping the pot manageable.

I have top pair with a King kicker (Tc Ks) on a board of 3h-4d-Td-6s-7h. I have led every street so far (pre-flop, flop, and turn), and Flashlight has called each time. The river 7h doesn't change much, though it completes a very unlikely straight (58). I believe my hand is still the best, and I want to extract one last bit of value from weaker Tens like JT, T9, or T8, or potentially middle pairs like 88 or 99. A bet of 1200 (half the pot) is a standard value sizing that keeps the pot manageable while still getting paid by worse hands.

The board runout [3h 4d Td 6s 7h] is extremely connected, completing straights for any 5, 8, or 9 combinations, in addition to any pair that Flash might have held. I have King-high, which loses to almost any pair or made straight. Flash has bet all three streets, representing a polarized range or a made hand. Given the strength of the board and the likelihood that Flash is value betting a made hand or a marginal pair that still beats my King-high, there is no justification for a call here. I must fold to avoid losing additional chips.

In 3-handed play, T9o is a strong hand to open from the button. I want to put pressure on the blinds, particularly the shorter stack in the Big Blind, and potentially take down the pot immediately or play a pot with initiative. A standard opening raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and provides a good risk-to-reward ratio.

Jack-three suited is a very weak hand, even with potential flush possibilities. Being in the small blind and out of position against an aggressive button raiser, calling is -EV. I prefer to preserve my stack and wait for a stronger starting hand where I can exert more control or have better equity.

Facing a 3x raise from the Button while out of position in the Big Blind. While 54s is a nice suited connector, calling a 3x raise out of position with a 56 BB stack is unprofitable. The sizing makes the pot odds worse than against a min-raise, and playing this hand out of position often leads to difficult post-flop spots. Folding is the most solid option.
9-8 offsuit is a weak starting hand even in a 3-handed game on the button. With plenty of chips and aggressive opponents, it is prudent to fold and wait for a stronger holding to enter the pot.

I am in the Small Blind with 92 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With no investment beyond the small blind and a player still left to act behind me, folding is the only profitable long-term play.

I am on the Button with a weak suited/offsuit Ace (Ah 3d). With 56 big blinds and both opponents in the blinds having much larger stacks, I want to try and steal the blinds. Raising to 2.5x (250) is a standard open from the Button to apply pressure while minimizing risk.

My hand (7d, 3h) is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. I am facing a raise from the button while sitting in the small blind, which is the worst post-flop position. Given the stack sizes and the trashy nature of my cards, there is no reason to defend this blind. I will fold and preserve my chips.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d-Td, which is a playable suited hand. The pot is offering good odds (400 to call 150), and it is a standard defensive play to call here against a button raise. Raising is an option, but calling allows me to see a flop with a decent hand while maintaining a controlled pot size.

I am out of position in the Big Blind against the Button raiser on a coordinated board (Q-9-8). My middle pair with a gutshot straight draw is a decent hand, but I should play cautiously and check to the original aggressor to evaluate their continuation, keeping the pot manageable.

The flop Qd 9s 8h connects heavily with the Big Blind's pre-flop calling range, containing many straight draws, pairs, and two-pairs. My hand, Ah 3d, has very little equity with just Ace-high and no draws. A continuation bet is unlikely to fold out better hands or draws and will mostly inflate the pot when I am behind. Checking behind to realize equity for free is the best play.

I have flopped an open-ended straight draw and on the turn, the Jack completed my straight (8-9-10-J-Q). This is a very strong hand, though I must be aware that a player holding a King would have a higher straight. Given the check-check on the flop, neither player showed immediate aggression, so I should now bet for value to build the pot and get paid by hands like two pairs or lower-ranking pairs.

The board is extremely dangerous with a 4-liner to a straight (Q-J-9-8), and Flashlight has led out on the turn. Holding only Ace-high with no straight or flush draws, I have virtually zero equity against a betting range. Calling or raising here is purely spew, so a fold is the only logical action.

I am on the Button with pocket eights (8s 8c), which is a very strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I want to raise for value and to exert pressure on the blinds. My standard opening raise size has been 300 throughout the tournament, and I will maintain that consistency here.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 6d, which is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Flash has been aggressive, and calling a raise of 300 into a 450 pot while out of position against an aggressive button is not ideal with such a weak holding. It is better to fold and wait for a more favorable spot to enter the pot.

With 9c 8h in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button, our hand is too weak and disconnected to call profitably out of position. I'll fold and wait for a better opportunity to defend or attack.

I am on the button with Q7o. While this is a weak hand, in a three-handed game, the button has the positional advantage and I am looking to steal the blinds. I will make a standard opening raise of 300 to apply pressure.

With pocket Kings, the second-best starting hand, I have a clear 3-bet for value against the Button's open. By raising to 900, I build a substantial pot pre-flop while isolating the raiser, leaving a comfortable stack-to-pot ratio for post-flop play.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Queen offsuit. Flashlight (BTN) opened to 300, and Pro (SB) has 3-bet to 900. While KQo is a strong starting hand, it is often dominated in 3-bet pots, particularly when the 3-bet comes from the Small Blind against a Button open. Pro has a relatively short stack (roughly 44BB), and his 3-bet sizing (900) suggests a strong range. If I call, I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand against one or both opponents, and I could easily be dominated by hands like AK, AQ, AA, KK, or QQ. Given the tournament context and the risk of being squeezed or dominated, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack.

I opened the pot with Q7o from the button, a standard steal attempt. Pro 3-bet to 900 from the small blind, which indicates a strong hand or a significant bluff. Q7o is too weak to continue against a 3-bet, as it is dominated by most of Pro's continuing range and performs poorly in a 3-bet pot. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play here.
Queen-Six offsuit is a very weak starting hand. From the Button with two players left to act and given my stack size, there's no reason to get involved. The best action is to fold.

In a blind-versus-blind situation after the button folds, 53s is a speculative suited hand that performs well enough to see a flop. Completing the small blind allows me to play the pot without bloating it out of position, while keeping the price low to realize my hand's equity and potentially outplay the opponent post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with a weak hand (8c 5c). Flash has completed the small blind to 100 total. Since I am already in the big blind, checking allows me to see the flop for free. Raising is not advisable with such a marginal holding.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4) on an Ace-high board in a limped pot. Since the pot is small and I am out of position, checking is the most standard and low-risk play to see if I can catch my straight or a cheap turn card.

I am in the big blind with an open-ended straight draw (5-8 on a 6-7-A board). Since the small blind (Flash) checked to me, I have the option to take a free card or place a bet. Given the small pot size and my draw, checking to see the turn is the most disciplined and effective way to realize my equity without overcommitting to a marginal hand.

On a paired board of 6s-7d-As-7s in a limped pot where the flop was checked around, my 5h-3h has very little value. I still have a gutshot straight draw to a 4, but the board pairing makes the hand riskier. I will check to see if I can get a free look at the river, as betting into a wide range that could easily contain a 7 or an Ace (or simply a better high card) is not profitable here.

I am in the big blind with an open-ended straight draw (5-6-7-8). The board is 6-7-A-7. Checking allows me to see the river for free, which is optimal for realizing my equity without inflating the pot unnecessarily on a paired board where I currently only hold high-card potential.

On the river, the board is 6s-7d-As-7s-3s, putting four spades on the table and pairing the sevens. I have made a second pair (Sevens and Threes with an Ace kicker). While this hand has some showdown value in a limped pot that has been checked all the way, it is extremely vulnerable to any single spade (giving the opponent a flush), any Ace (higher two pair), or any Seven (trips). There is no value in betting here, as better hands won't fold and worse hands are unlikely to call. Checking is the most prudent action to reach showdown cheaply or evaluate if Flashlight decides to bluff.

I am in the big blind with 8-high (8c 5c) on a board of 6s 7d As 7s 3s. The opponent has checked. My hand has no showdown value, and betting would essentially be a pure bluff into a checked-down pot, which is unlikely to succeed or provide value given the low pot size and lack of equity. Checking to see the showdown is the only logical decision.

I am on the button with 8-7 offsuit, which is a marginal but playable hand in a 3-handed game for a steal attempt. Both opponents have folded to my button raises frequently in recent hands. I will maintain my standard opening size of 300 to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot pre-flop.

I am in the small blind with a pair of deuces, which is a classic set-mining hand. Given that the button has opened, calling allows me to see a flop with reasonable pot odds to potentially hit a set, while keeping the pot manageable out of position. Raising here could be risky given the current stack depths and the fact that the big blind is still to act, so I will call to proceed.

Pocket 4s is a great set-mining hand. With the button raising and the small blind calling, I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 200 to win 700) to see a flop. The implied odds are also very good since there are two players in the pot and my stack depth is sufficient. I will call and evaluate the flop, continuing only if I hit a set or get a very favorable board.

I am first to act on a flop of Queen, 3, 5. My pair of deuces is currently an underpair to the board and very vulnerable. While I have a backdoor straight draw and a low pair, there is no value in betting into two opponents. Checking is the standard play here to see how the action unfolds. I will likely fold to any significant aggression given the board texture and the number of players involved.

I have pocket 4s on a Qc 3h 5c board. I missed my set and only have a marginal third pair. The board is wet with a club flush draw and an overcard. Being out of position against two opponents, including the pre-flop raiser who has yet to act, the best course of action is to check and prepare to fold to any significant aggression.

I am on the button with 8-7 offsuit on a Qc-3h-5c flop. I was the pre-flop aggressor. Both opponents have checked to me. While I have missed this board, a continuation bet is a standard play here to take down the pot, as this board likely missed most of their calling range. However, with two opponents, I'll check back to keep the pot small and see if I can improve on the turn or take it away later if they show more weakness.

The board shows Queen, 3, 5, Ace. I am in the small blind holding a pair of deuces. The action is currently on Flash (the button), who has not yet acted on the turn after I and Pro have checked. Since I have already checked and it is not currently my turn to act, I am technically waiting. However, given the requirement to provide an action, I will reaffirm my check to stay in the hand as passively as possible given my weak holding and the overcards on the board.

The turn card brings an Ace and completes the club flush draw, while my hand is only an underpair to the board's high cards with no club blocker. Betting here would be a pure bluff into two opponents on a very wet board. The best and safest action is to check and prepare to fold if faced with a bet.

I am on the button and was the pre-flop aggressor. The board is Qc 3h 5c Ac. After the flop checked around, the turn brings the Ace of Clubs, which is an excellent scare card that favors my perceived range. Both opponents have checked again, showing significant weakness. Since I have 8-high and no showdown value, this is a prime opportunity to take the pot with a semi-bluff. A bet here represents an Ace or a potential flush draw that improved, and it's unlikely either opponent can call without at least a pair of Queens or better.

The turn card Ace of Clubs provides significant overcards (Ace, Queen) to my pair of deuces. Given the board texture of Queen-Three-Five-Ace, my deuces are extremely weak and unlikely to be the best hand. Facing a bet of 500 into a 900 pot from the button, there is no profitable way to continue. I am folding to avoid losing more chips on a hand with zero showdown value.

I am facing a bet on a very wet board featuring an Ace, a Queen, and three clubs. My pocket fours are essentially a bluff catcher with no drawing potential and no club to block the flush. As planned in my previous thought process, folding to any aggression is the only logical choice here.

I am on the button with 7-5 offsuit, which is a very weak hand with no playability or equity. In a 3-handed game, preserving my stack is more important than attempting a steal with such a low-value hand. Therefore, folding is the correct strategic decision.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit after the Button folded. This is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative, extract value from weaker hands, and potentially just win the pot right now.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. I have direct position on Pro for the remainder of the hand and I am receiving 2-to-1 odds to call. Given my stack lead and the playability of this hand in position, calling to see a flop is a standard and profitable move.

The flop comes K-9-9 with two clubs. While I missed the flop, this board texture is highly favorable for my perceived pre-flop raising range, which contains many Kings. I also hold the Ace of Clubs, which gives me backdoor flush potential and blocks the nut club draw from my opponent. A small continuation bet of 1/3 pot is ideal here to apply pressure on the Big Blind, fold out their random equity (like small pocket pairs or lower Ace-highs), and capitalize on my range advantage.

On a board of Kc-9c-9h, I have flopped trips with a 10 kicker. The Small Blind (Pro) has led out with a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 600. While raising is an option to build a pot, the board is fairly dry (aside from club draws) and a call allows Pro to continue bluffing with air or value-betting weaker hands on later streets. Given the stack sizes and Pro's relatively short stack (under 50BB), I want to keep their range as wide as possible. I will call this bet and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn brings a completely blank 2d. My opponent called my flop c-bet on a paired, two-club board, indicating they likely have a piece of it, such as a King, a Nine, a middle pocket pair, or a club flush draw. Continuing to bluff here with Ace-high is risky and would be building a pot out of position against a range that may not fold easily. By checking, I control the pot size and can still win at showdown against missed flush draws (which I heavily block with the Ace of clubs), or re-evaluate if my opponent decides to bet.

On the turn, the board is Kc-9c-9h-2d and I have trip nines. Pro checked after betting the flop, which often indicates a hand like a King that is now in pot-control mode or a complete air hand. Since I have a very strong hand, I want to extract value from a King or any draws (like clubs) that might be out there. A bet of 500 into the 1000 pot is a standard value-betting size that remains small enough for a King or a pocket pair to potentially call or for Pro to occasionally check-raise bluff.

The opponent called my flop continuation bet and is now firing a half-pot bet on the turn after I showed weakness by checking. While I hold the Ace of Clubs, which blocks the nut flush draw, it also means my opponent's range is heavily weighted toward made hands like a King, a Nine, or a middle pocket pair, rather than a missed draw. My Ace-Jack high has very little equity against this value range. Calling out of position with just Ace-high and no strong draw is a losing play, and check-raising as a bluff is far too risky. The most prudent action is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with pocket fives (5s 5h). This is a solid hand to open-raise with to either steal the blinds or play in position, hoping to flop a set. A standard raise to 2.5x or 3x the big blind is appropriate here.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Nine suited, a solid starting hand in a three-handed game. The Button (Pro) has opened for a standard raise of 2.5x (250). Folding would be too tight given the strength of the hand. While 3-betting is a viable aggressive option to take control of the pot, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has good playability and preserves my chip lead without ballooning the pot out of position. If the Big Blind (Flashlight) calls as well, the hand plays well multi-way. I will call the additional 200.

My hand, 8c 4d, is extremely weak, unsuited, and disconnected. Although the pot odds provide some incentive to call, playing this hand out of position against two opponents—one of whom is the button—will likely lead to a difficult and low-equity post-flop scenario. Given that I have a healthy stack and it is still early enough in the tournament, folding is the most disciplined and conservative approach to avoid hemorrhaging chips with a dominated hand.

On this coordinated board of 6s-8s-5c, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7 to make a 9-high straight) and one overcard (the Jack). As the caller out of position, checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) is the standard play. This allows me to see how Pro reacts to this board, which likely misses a lot of his high-card button-raising range. If he checks back, I get a free turn to try and hit my straight or improve. If he bets, I can evaluate the sizing to see if it's worth continuing with my draw and overcard.

I flopped bottom set on a very wet, connected board (6s 8s 5c) that features multiple straight draws and a flush draw. With my opponent checking to me, I must bet for value and protection to charge any draws like spade flush draws or open-ended straight draws (like 79 or 47). A half-pot bet builds the pot while extracting value from weaker pairs and charging the draws an appropriate price.

On this 6s-8s-5c flop, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing any 7 to make the nut straight, 5-6-7-8-9) and one overcard (the Jack). Pro, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 300 into a 600 pot. This 1/2 pot bet offers me 3:1 odds (calling 300 to win a total pot of 1200), which is 25%. While my immediate equity with just the gutshot is roughly 8.5% for the turn, the implied odds of hitting a hidden straight on this board are significant, especially against a button-raising range that may contain overpairs or strong top pairs. Additionally, my Jack could be an out if Pro is betting with a hand like KQ or AJ, and I have a backdoor club draw. Calling allows me to see a turn card cheaply and evaluate. If the turn is a 7, I will have the nuts; if it's a club or a high card, I may find opportunities to take the pot away later.

On the turn, the Treys of Spades completes a potential flush draw and some low straights. My hand (Jack-high with a gutshot straight draw) has not improved significantly. Acting first out of position, I will check to Pro, the pre-flop and flop aggressor. This allows me to see how he reacts to the flush card and potentially see a free river card if he checks back, or evaluate the price of a bet if he continues his aggression.

The 3s on the turn completes the obvious spade flush draw and adds to the straight possibilities on a highly connected 6-8-5-3 board. Although I have a set of fives, it is very vulnerable here. Checking behind provides pot control, avoids getting check-raised by completed flushes or straights, and allows me to see a free river where I might improve to a full house.

On this river, the board has paired the three, and a flush is possible. I have Jack-high with Jc 9c, which has almost zero showdown value against Pro's button-opening range. Pro checked back on the turn, which suggests he either has a marginal made hand (like an 8, 6, 5, or Ace-high) or has given up on a bluff. While I could attempt to bluff here to represent the flush or a straight, Pro's remaining stack is relatively small and he might feel priced in to call with any pair. Since I am the chip leader, I will check and concede the pot if he has a better hand, rather than risking more chips on a low-equity bluff.

The river brings the 3d, pairing the board and upgrading my set of fives to a full house (fives full of threes). The only hands that beat me are pocket sixes or eights, which likely would have raised the flop, or an unlikely 63/83. Flash checking the river indicates they may have a completed flush, a straight, or a one-pair hand that is wary of the paired board. I must bet for value. A bet of around half to two-thirds pot should get looked up by flushes and straights that cannot find a fold.

On this board of 6s-8s-5c-3s-3d, I have Jack-high (Jc 9c). Pro raised pre-flop, bet the flop, checked the turn, and has now bet 800 into a 1200 pot on the river. His turn check suggests he either had a marginal hand that didn't want to face a check-raise on a flush-completing card, or he was giving up with a bluff. His river bet represents value (a flush, a straight, or a pair of 8s/6s/5s) or a polarized bluff. Since I have no showdown value and Jack-high is unlikely to beat even most of his bluffs (which would often include Ax or Qx), there is no reason to call. As the chip leader, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 7s3s. This is a speculative suited connector, but 7-3 is very weak even with the flush potential. Looking at the stacks, Pro has a relatively short stack (5425) while I (13275) and Flashlight (11150) are deeper. In a 3-handed game, the button is powerful, but this hand is below the threshold for a standard raise or even a speculative call given the potential for being dominated or facing a 3-bet. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (T5o) against the big blind (Pro). Given that Pro is playing aggressively and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, playing a weak hand is likely to lead to difficult post-flop decisions. Folding allows me to preserve my stack and wait for a better equity spot.

I am in the button position with Jack-Eight offsuit. This is a weak hand with poor playability, especially in a three-handed game where I would have to play post-flop against the blinds. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a more advantageous hand.

With King-Six suited in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a solid hand to attack the Big Blind. A raise to 2.5x (250) is my standard sizing from this position to either steal the blinds immediately or take the initiative into the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 250. Given the positional dynamics (I will be in position post-flop) and the strength of an Ace-high hand in a blind-vs-blind battle, this is a clear continuing hand. Calling is a solid play here to see a flop without bloating the pot, allowing me to utilize my positional advantage against Pro's 53 BB stack.

I have top pair with a good kicker (King) on a relatively dry, low board (2c 6h 4s). Betting here is standard to extract value from worse pairs (like a 4, a 2, or smaller pocket pairs like 33, 55) and to protect my equity against random overcards or gutshot straight draws. A half-pot sizing of 250 works perfectly to balance getting value while maintaining pot control.

On a flop of 2c-6h-4s, I have top pair with an Ace kicker. My opponent (Pro) has made a standard continuation bet of 250 into a 500 pot. Given that Pro raised from the Small Blind, their range is wide. My hand is very strong in this context, but raising now might only serve to fold out their bluffs or weaker hands. By calling, I keep their range wide, allow them to continue bluffing on future streets, and maintain control of the pot while in position.

The Queen on the turn is an overcard to my pair of sixes, reducing my hand to middle pair. Betting here is risky as it will likely only be called by better hands (such as a Queen, higher pocket pairs, or better sixes) and strong draws. Checking allows me to control the pot size with a marginal made hand, keeps his bluffing range wide (such as missed straight draws like 53 or A5), and lets me evaluate Flash's reaction.

I have second pair with an Ace kicker on a board of 2c-6h-4s-Qh. Pro, who raised pre-flop and c-bet the flop, has checked the turn. My hand has significant showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value against a range that likely includes some better pairs or a Queen. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, maintain my position, and can evaluate the river action. This also avoids potentially being check-raised by a better hand or a semi-bluff.

The 5d on the river completes a potential straight for any 3 and puts four to a straight on the board (2-4-5-6). My pair of sixes has degraded to a marginal bluff-catcher. Betting here has no value, as worse hands will almost certainly fold, and I will only be called or raised by better hands like a straight, a Queen, or two pair. Checking allows me to control the pot, potentially reach a cheap showdown, and let Flash check behind with missed draws, Ace-high, or smaller pairs.

On the river with a board of 2c-6h-4s-Qh-5d, I have a pair of sixes with an Ace kicker. The action went: Pro raised pre-flop, I called. Pro bet the flop and I called. Both Pro and I checked the turn and river. Pro's checks on the turn and river suggest a hand with some showdown value that is not strong enough to value bet, or a missed hand like AK or AJ. My pair of sixes has significant showdown value in this blind-vs-blind scenario. Betting now is unlikely to get called by worse hands or to fold out better hands. Therefore, checking back to realize my equity and take the hand to showdown is the most prudent play.

With Qs8s on the Button, I have a suited gapper which is strong enough to attempt a blind steal. Raising to 250 gives me a good chance to win the pot pre-flop while also allowing me to play in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 4h. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. While 6-4 suited has some speculative value as a suited connector, it is quite weak to play out of position against a button raise, especially with a relatively short-stacked opponent who might be looking to commit chips. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting as a bluff is risky given the stack sizes and previous dynamics. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

I hold a strong pocket pair, Tens, which is a premium hand in a heads-up situation. Since I am the big stack and the small blind has already folded, I want to take the initiative and build a pot against the Button (Pro). A 3-bet is a standard, aggressive play here to extract value or take down the pot pre-flop. Raising to 800 is consistent with my previous aggressive play style and puts pressure on the opponent.

I opened with a suited gapper on the button to steal the blinds, but faced a significant 3-bet from the Big Blind. Qs8s is not strong enough to call a 3-bet, as it will often be dominated by the opponent's stronger queens or better flush draws, and lacks the raw equity to continue. Folding is the optimal choice.

I am the chip leader in a 3-handed game. Q8o is a standard opening hand from the button in this format to apply pressure to the blinds. My consistent strategy has been to raise to 300 from the button to pick up the blinds or play the hand in position.

I am holding Queen-Nine offsuit in the small blind against a button open. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. With a stack of 11,400, I do not need to take risks on speculative hands in a disadvantageous position. Folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision here.
K5o is a weak, disconnected, offsuit hand. Facing a 3x open from the Button while out of position, the best decision is to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

My hand, 8c 5d, is very weak and disconnected, making it unsuitable for an opening raise or a call from the button. With 11,400 chips, I have a healthy stack and should play disciplined, avoiding marginal hands to minimize unnecessary risk.

I am in the small blind with a weak offsuit hand, J6o. Flashlight folded on the button. Since I am out of position against the big blind (Flash) and hold a poor starting hand, the most fundamentally sound decision is to fold rather than try to steal or complete the blind.

With A6 offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game, this is a borderline steal hand. However, my stack is around 47 big blinds, and my opponents have large stacks and have been playing aggressively, often 3-betting or floating pre-flop. Playing A6o post-flop can be tricky and usually results in losing a medium pot when dominated. I will fold and look for a better spot to commit chips.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Six offsuit. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). While Q6o has some potential in a blind-versus-blind battle, it is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Given that I am currently the chip leader and want to maintain a disciplined approach without taking unnecessary risks in small pots, I will choose to fold this hand rather than calling or raising and potentially getting into a difficult post-flop situation against an active opponent.

Holding King-Six suited on the button, I have a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. My stack is the largest at the table, allowing me to apply pressure to the blinds. Pro is relatively short-stacked at 45BB. A 3x raise is my standard opening size which should either take the pot down pre-flop or allow me to play a pot in position against one of the blinds.

I am in the small blind with 8h-Js, which is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Flash has raised from the button, and calling 250 chips with this hand would likely put me in a difficult spot post-flop against a wider range. Given my stack size and the strength of my hand, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my chips.
I am in the Big Blind with 2c 6s, which is an extremely weak and disconnected hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, folding is the only standard and profitable play.

I am on the button with suited connector cards, Queen-Ten suited. This is a strong hand in a late position. Opening the pot with a raise to 300 chips is a standard strategic move to apply pressure on the blinds, build a pot, and potentially secure initiative.

Facing a 3x open from the Button while in the Small Blind. Although A6s has some playability and makes for a decent 3-bet bluff candidate, my stack is somewhat short at 45 BBs, and playing a bloated pot out of position or facing a 4-bet would be detrimental. Folding is a safe, standard decision to preserve chips for better spots.

In a 3-handed game, defending the Big Blind with 8-7 offsuit against a Button open is standard. I am getting excellent pot odds, needing to call only 200 chips to compete for a total pot of 650 (approx 3.25 to 1). While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the connectivity of the cards and the price offered make a call the most appropriate strategy to see a flop without over-committing against the other big stack.

I have 8-high on an Ace-King high board after defending the big blind. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, which includes many Ax, Kx, and broadway combinations. I have no made hand and no real drawing equity. My plan is to check and fold to any significant bet, as there is little merit in bluffing into a range that likely connected with this board.

I am on the button with a Queen-high flush draw on an Ace-King-Nine board. This board texture heavily favors the big blind, as they likely have many Aces and Kings in their defending range. While I have the initiative, a continuation bet into this board is risky as I am unlikely to get folds from an Ace or King, and I could be check-raised. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, control the pot size, and potentially improve my hand on the turn without bloating the pot with a drawing hand.

On the turn, the board has paired with nines alongside an Ace and a King. I have no made hand and no draw. Since I am first to act on this street, checking is the most logical play. I have no equity to justify a bet, and bluffing into a board that contains an Ace and a King is generally unwise against a pre-flop aggressor unless I have a specific read. I will check and fold to any subsequent bet.

The board has paired nines on the turn, and checking behind on the flop with my flush draw was a reasonable defensive play. With Flash checking the turn again, they likely have a weak hand, a missed draw, or are trapping. Betting now applies pressure and builds the pot in case I hit my flush on the river, or potentially takes the pot immediately if they are weak. A bet of 350 into a 650 pot is a standard sizing to accomplish this.
